ctx.name: Carlos
ctx.notes: For Carlos, this performance conversation needs to make the quality bar concrete and usable. I want to understand whether prior feedback translated into behavior changes or stayed abstract. I also need to hear how he defines good work right now and where he feels blocked in growth. Without that shared baseline, feedback can feel subjective and demotivating. My goal is to align on specific expectations, examples, and one near-term development focus he can practice immediately.

focusPoints.label: Make the quality bar concrete with examples.
focusPoints.reason: What prior feedback changed in his day-to-day output, or stayed abstract. Could be a gap in interpretation, or just missing a clear “good vs not good” example—worth clarifying with specific artifacts and outcomes.

focusPoints.label: How Carlos defines “good work” right now.
focusPoints.reason: Whether he can articulate the bar for his level in a way you can observe and measure. At Junior in a performance conversation, shared definitions prevent feedback from feeling subjective and demotivating.

focusPoints.label: Where he feels blocked in growth.
focusPoints.reason: What he thinks is limiting his next step right now, and what would make progress feel real. Could be skill gaps, feedback loop timing, or unclear expectations—worth surfacing so you can pick one near-term practice.

focusPoints.label: Feedback landing — both directions.
focusPoints.reason: How critique conversations have been landing: whether his revisions actually reflect the feedback, and whether he’s getting the kind of specificity he needs. This keeps performance feedback actionable rather than just evaluative.

transcript.question: Before I share my view, how do you think the last stretch has gone?
transcript.answer: I can name wins, but I am not always sure they match the team bar.

transcript.question: name wins, always — can you say more about what that means for you right now?
transcript.answer: Feedback has helped, though I still miss consistency under time pressure.

transcript.question: Under time pressure, where does the consistency slip show up most clearly?
transcript.answer: I sometimes focus on polish and lose sight of the core message.

transcript.question: When you lose the core message, what are you missing in the brief?
transcript.answer: I want clearer examples of what excellent junior-level work looks like here.

transcript.question: Where do you think you've fallen short of where you want to be?
transcript.answer: When reviews are broad, I am not sure what to change first.

transcript.question: When reviews are broad, what specifically is missing for you?
transcript.answer: I would like to grow faster in concept rationale, not just execution speed.

transcript.question: Concept rationale — what makes that harder for you right now?
transcript.answer: A tighter loop on one skill at a time would help me improve.

transcript.question: What would a tighter one-skill feedback loop look like in practice?
transcript.answer: If we align on concrete criteria, I can work against them confidently.

transcript.question: What one habit do you want to practice before we meet again?
transcript.answer: I will start by writing one explicit priority rule I will use when time pressure hits this month.
